1: Standard address formula opening the Holiness Code proper. Chapter 18 begins with a framing preamble (Leviticus 18:1-5) before listing the sexual prohibitions. The placement of the sexual ethics code immediately after the blood/sacrifice laws (Leviticus 17) signals that purity of body, like purity of sacrifice, is part of the holiness program.
2: ani YHWH Eloheikhem: 'I am YHWH your God.' This self-identification formula appears throughout Leviticus 18-26 as a refrain anchoring each law in YHWH's own identity and covenant relationship. The laws are not arbitrary rules but expressions of who YHWH is and what the covenant relationship requires. The formula occurs 48 times in Leviticus, clustering in the Holiness Code.
3: kema'asei erets-Mitsrayim asher yeshavtem bah lo ta'asu ukema'asei erets-Kena'an: 'You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived, and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan.' Israel's sexual ethics are defined negatively against both past (Egypt) and future (Canaan) environments. The logic: Israel is not to assimilate to surrounding cultures. The double prohibition creates Israel's ethical identity as a people genuinely distinct from both its origin and its destination.
4: et-mishpatai ta'asu ve'et-ḥuqqotai tishemru lalekhet bahem: 'Keep my rules and observe my statutes and walk in them.' Two legal categories: mishpatim (judgments/rules — laws whose rationale is accessible to reason) and ḥuqqot (statutes — laws whose rationale is YHWH's sovereign decree). Both require obedience; the distinction is not between easier and harder laws but between transparent and opaque ones. Lalekhet bahem (to walk in them) — the covenant life as a path, not a set of discrete acts.
5: ushmartem et-ḥuqqotai ve'et-mishpatai asher ya'aseh otam ha'adam vaḥai bahem: 'Keep my statutes and my rules; the person who does them shall live by them.' Vaḥai bahem (and he shall live by them) — the life-promise of the covenant commands. Paul quotes this verse in Galatians 3:12 and Romans 10:5 to distinguish law-observance from faith: the law promises life to those who do all its commands; faith promises life to those who trust. Paul's point is not that the law was wrong in its life-promise but that no one fully does all its commands — only Christ did. Leviticus 18:5 is the positive statement of what covenant obedience achieves; Galatians 3:10-13 is Paul's analysis of why it cannot be achieved without Christ.
6: kol-ish ish el-kol-she'er besaro lo tiqrevu legallot erva: 'None of you shall approach any close relative to uncover nakedness.' She'er basar (close relative, lit. 'flesh relative') — the term for those sharing kinship bonds. Erva (nakedness/genitalia) — the consistent term throughout Leviticus 18 for the sexual domain. The root glh (to uncover/expose) when used with erva means to engage in sexual relations. Lo tiqrevu (you shall not approach) uses the same approach-language as the sanctuary access rules — sexual impropriety violates sacred space.
7: First specific prohibition: father's sexual partner (mother). Erva avikha ve'erva immekha (the nakedness of your father and the nakedness of your mother) — the mother's body is described as 'your father's nakedness,' reflecting the marital one-flesh bond: to approach her is to violate your father. The double identification (she is your mother; she is your father's nakedness) closes both possible loopholes.
8: erva eshet avikha lo tegalle: 'You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife.' This covers step-mothers and other wives beyond one's birth mother — the prohibition extends to anyone in the role of father's wife. Erva avikha hi (it is your father's nakedness) — the same logic as Leviticus 18:7: the wife's body participates in the marital bond's sanctity.
9: Prohibitions on half-sisters (paternal and maternal, born inside or outside the family household). The comprehensive listing ('whether born in the house or elsewhere') closes kinship loopholes — adoption, mixed family structures, and children born outside wedlock are all included. The Holiness Code's sexual legislation is notably more comprehensive than earlier prohibitions in the Torah.
10: Granddaughters (son's daughter or daughter's daughter) are prohibited. Ki ervatekha henah (for they are your own nakedness) — the granddaughter participates in the grandfather's identity through descent, making incest a form of self-violation. The kinship logic: you are diminished by what happens to those who share your flesh.
11: The daughter of one's father's wife (step-sister through the father) — 'begotten by your father; she is your sister.' The prohibition covers relationships that might be considered distant enough to be permitted in some ancient Near Eastern contexts (e.g., Abraham's marriage to Sarah, who was his half-sister, Genesis 20:12, which predates this legislation).
12: Father's sister — she'er avikha hi (she is your father's blood relative). The she'er (flesh) language grounds the prohibition in kinship: she shares your father's body, which makes her body an extension of your own family identity. The prohibition extends the incest boundary to the parental generation's siblings.
13: Mother's sister — the same reasoning. Ki she'er immekha hi (she is your mother's flesh). The symmetry between paternal and maternal prohibitions (Leviticus 18:12-13) ensures no distinction between the two lines of descent.
14: Father's brother's wife (aunt by marriage): you shall not approach his wife. Dedat avikha hi (she is your aunt) — the specific term dodah (aunt) appears here. The prohibition on the uncle's wife extends the sexual boundary to affinal (marriage-based) as well as consanguineal (blood-based) relationships.
15: Daughter-in-law: eshet binekha hi (she is your son's wife). Lo tegalle ervatah (you shall not uncover her nakedness) — this case is also mentioned in the Judah/Tamar narrative (Gen 38), where Judah unknowingly sleeps with his daughter-in-law. The Levitical prohibition makes what was transgressive in Genesis into an explicit legal offense.
16: Brother's wife. Erva aḥikha hi (it is your brother's nakedness) — parallel to the father-wife logic. The notable exception to this rule is the levirate law (Deuteronomy 25:5-10), which commands a man to marry his brother's widow when the brother died childless; Leviticus 18:16 prohibits this relationship in normal circumstances, while Deuteronomy creates a specific exception for line-preservation purposes.
17: erva isha uvittah lo tegalle: 'You shall not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter.' The prohibition on simultaneous relations with mother and daughter is absolute — zimmah hi (it is depravity). The term zimmah (depravity/wickedness) marks the most egregious violations within the sexual code. The prohibition extends to granddaughters in both directions.
18: ve'isha el-aḥotah lo tiqqaḥ litsror: 'You shall not take a woman as a rival wife to her sister.' Tsarah (rival wife/adversary) is the technical term for a co-wife in a polygynous marriage. The prohibition applies only while both sisters are alive — be'ḥayyeiha (in her lifetime). This directly addresses the situation of Jacob's marriages to Leah and Rachel (Gen 29-30), who were biological sisters and produced the conflict that tsarah implies.
19: Sexual relations during menstruation (niddah). The prohibition connects the sexual laws with the broader purity system: menstruation generates ritual impurity (Leviticus 15:19-24), and sexual contact during that time compounds the impurity. The niddah category bridges the sexual ethics of Leviticus 18 and the bodily-discharge purity laws of Leviticus 15.
20: ve'el-eshet amitekha lo titten shekhovtekha lezara: 'You shall not have sexual relations with your neighbor's wife, defiling yourself with her.' Amitekha (your neighbor/fellow Israelite) — the covenant community term. Lezara (for impurity/defilement) — the sexual violation is framed as a form of contamination parallel to ritual impurity. This is also the seventh commandment (lo tin'af, Exodus 20:14), here given in its Levitical Holiness Code formulation.
21: umiZar'akha lo-titten leha'avir laMolech: 'You shall not give any of your offspring to be offered to Molech.' Molech (also vocalized Molek) is the deity associated with child sacrifice, practiced in Canaan and apparently sometimes in Israel (2 Kings 16:3; Jeremiah 32:35). Leha'avir (to cause to pass through) is the technical term for child sacrifice in the OT. velo teḥallel et-shem Elohekha: 'do not profane the name of your God' — child sacrifice is framed as a desecration of YHWH's name, not merely a social atrocity. The insertion of the Molech prohibition within the sexual ethics code reflects its association with fertility-cult worship.
22: ve'et-zakar lo tishkav mishkevei isha to'evah hi: 'You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination.' To'evah (abomination/detestable thing) is a strong term of categorical rejection, used elsewhere for idolatry (Deuteronomy 7:25) and child sacrifice (Deuteronomy 12:31). The verse prohibits male same-sex intercourse in terms drawn from the sexual categories of Lev 18 as a whole (mishkevei isha, literally 'lyings of a woman' = the manner of lying with a woman).
23: uvekol-behema lo-titten shekhovtekha letame'ah bah: 'You shall not have sexual relations with any animal, defiling yourself with it.' Tevel (perversion/violation of created order) — the term used for bestiality; it conveys disorder at the level of creation categories, not merely moral transgression. The female parallel is explicit: a woman must not stand before an animal for sexual purposes. Both are tevel — violations of the created order of kinds.
24: al-tittame'u bekhol-elleh: 'Do not defile yourselves by any of these things.' Nitme'u (became defiled) applies to the Canaanites — the land's former inhabitants who practiced these things and were expelled by YHWH. The defiling practices are not merely matters of cultural preference; they generated genuine moral-ritual impurity that affected the land itself.
25: vatitma ha'arets vaeafqod avonah aleiha vataqqi ha'arets et-yoshveiha: 'The land became defiled; I punished it for its iniquity, and the land vomited out its inhabitants.' The land as a moral agent: vataqqi (vomited out) — the land cannot tolerate sustained moral pollution and expels its inhabitants. This is not mythological; it is the theological explanation of Canaanite displacement. The same threat is directed at Israel in Leviticus 18:28 — the land has no ethnic loyalty; it vomits out whoever defiles it.
26: The obligation falls on Israel and on resident aliens (hager hagar betokham) equally — the sexual prohibitions are not merely Israel's cultural particularity but reflect moral norms applicable to all who live in the land. This extension to the alien is consistent with the Holiness Code's pattern of including non-Israelites in fundamental ethical obligations.
27: The Canaanites had committed all these abominations (to'evot) — hence their expulsion. The historical note grounds the prohibition in demonstrated moral-historical consequence: these practices bring divine judgment that results in national displacement. Israel is warned by precedent, not merely by abstract command.
28: velo-taqqi ha'arets etkhem betam'akhem otah: 'Lest the land vomit you out when you defile it, as it vomited out the nation that was before you.' The land's moral intolerance is turned as a warning against Israel: identical behavior will bring identical consequences regardless of covenant status. The exile of 722 BCE (Israel) and 586 BCE (Judah) vindicated this warning — the land did indeed 'vomit out' Israel when it persisted in these practices.
29: ki kol-asher ya'aseh mikkol-hatto'evot ha'elleh venikh'ru hanaefashot ha'osot: 'For anyone who commits any of these abominations — the persons who do them shall be cut off from among their people.' The karet penalty applies comprehensively across all the listed prohibitions. Karet (being cut off) in its most severe form may mean premature death at divine hands; in less severe applications it means exclusion from the covenant community.
30: ushmartem et-mishmarti: 'Keep my charge.' Mishmeret (charge/obligation, what is kept/guarded) — the term for the covenant responsibility of obedience. The closing exhortation frames the entire chapter: Israel is to guard against the practices that caused Canaan's expulsion. Ani YHWH Eloheikhem — the opening refrain closes the chapter, forming an envelope structure around the entire sexual ethics code.
